You are the DIRTY-ROOM INTEGRATOR (may read everything; must NOT transplant
logic from replaced files — write ADAPTERS: type conversion and data plumbing routing existing
entry points through the audited clean-room code). Where clean code lacks a behavior, KEEP the old
path (with its header) and report the gap precisely; never patch clean code with old logic, never
restore old logic outside kept files. Files you author carry NO license header. After finishing,
run: python3 scripts/gen_license_headers.py (from the repo root) so headers track the new
derivation surface, and validate with cargo build --workspace plus the test suites named below —
captured-HS parity fixtures MUST stay green; if a parity test fails, fix your ADAPTER only.
NAMING POLICY: integrated clean modules take natural suffix-free names; when you delete a ported
module, the clean replacement takes its name (precedent: crates/tamarin-parser/src/wf/).
Repo: /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs. Clean sources under /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/<cluster>/workspace/.
Append what you did to /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/INTEGRATION_REPORT.md. Return a 12-line
plain-text summary. No structured output.
TASK: complete the tamarin-server integration for units A (web handlers) and B (graph generation).
Clean sources: weblayer/workspace/web-clean (dispatch.rs Server<ProverOps> state machine, routes,
forms incl. dynamic textarea rows, templates) and graphdot/workspace/graph-clean (generation:
alloc/render/generate/options + serializer + abbreviation). Latest re-probe summary (may have
closed the simplification-level and wrap gaps — read graphdot BEHAVIOR.md newest section first):
${JSON.stringify((await Promise.resolve('')))} — ignore this placeholder; read the files.
1. Refresh the vendored copies in crates/tamarin-server/src/ from the clean workspaces (mechanical
   path fixes only). Rename per policy: after deleting the ported graph/ modules you replace,
   graph_clean -> graph; web_clean -> web.
2. GRAPH: route system_to_dot/system_to_dot_with/render_svg_or_dot_with through clean generation
   (adapter: ported System -> clean model). Delete handlers/dot.rs ported serialization,
   graph/abbreviation.rs, graph/repr.rs, graph/simplify.rs where the clean side now covers them
   (generation round-3 characterized content mapping; re-probe may cover simplification). Keep
   ported code ONLY for verified remaining gaps (e.g. wrap decision if still open) — report each.
3. WEB: implement the clean ProverOps trait as an adapter over the ported prover
   (parse/apply-method/autoprove/pretty fragments), route requests through web::dispatch, replace
   remaining theory_html.rs rendering and the non-local-origin/edit-form kept paths (textarea rows
   now solved; origin stays a parameterized hook — wire it to the real peer-address check).
   Delete theory_html.rs and ported handler logic that dispatch now covers.
4. Validate: cargo build --workspace; cargo test -p tamarin-server (all suites, parity fixtures
   green); cargo test -p tamarin-parser; the graph-clean opt-in corpus gates
   (GRAPHCLEAN_CORPUS=/home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/graphdot/oracle/dot_corpus). Then
   gen_license_headers.py and report the header-count delta.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content: # Dirty-room integration report — graph/dot + web clusters Date: 2026-07-17. Integrator: dirty-room (adapters only; no logic transplanted from replaced files into clean code). Repo: `/home/kamilner/tamarin-rs`. Precedent followed: the wellformedness cluster (`crates/tamarin-parser/src/wf/`) — clean sources vendored as an in-crate module with mechanical path fixes plus a small workspace-authored adapter; clean files carry no license header, kept ported files keep theirs. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ## 0. Vendoring (both clusters) — DONE Clean deliverables copied verbatim into the server crate as in-crate modules, with the single mechanical fix `crate::` -> `super::` (module path re-rooting) and — in `graph_clean/mod.rs` only — the module-doc doctest fence changed ```` ``` ```` -> ```` ```ignore ```` (it referenced `graph_clean::…` as an extern crate, which does not exist once vendored; the doctest is the clean crate's own, not a workspace test). No license headers added — these are the relicensable clean sources. * `crates/tamarin-server/src/graph_clean/` <- `graph-clean/src/` (`mod.rs` <- `lib.rs`, `model.rs`, `term.rs`, `abbrev.rs`, `dot.rs`) — 837 LOC. * `crates/tamarin-server/src/web_clean/` <- `web-clean/src/` (`mod.rs` <- `lib.rs`, `envelope`, `errors`, `escape`, `forms`, `intdot`, `page`, `proofscript`, `route`, `text`, `notfound_template`, `shell_template`) — 1005 LOC. * Wired `pub mod graph_clean; pub mod web_clean;` into `lib.rs` (headered file; only the two `pub mod` lines + an explanatory comment were added — the kept file's own header was not touched). Fidelity check: `sed 's/super::/crate::/' ` diffs byte-identical to each original clean source (and `mod.rs` modulo the doctest fence). The clean crates' own suites still pass at their workspace locations: `graph-clean` 16 round-trip + 7 abbrev + 1 doctest; `web-clean` parity suite. The vendored `web_clean` inline unit tests (19) run and pass inside `tamarin-server`. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ## 1. WEB cluster — REWIRED (partial, byte-safe) Adapters route prover-generated content as opaque pane/body strings into the clean templates. All routed paths are byte-identical to the ported output for the corpus/fixture theories, or a strictly-more-HS-faithful byte change that the (structural) parity suite accepts. REPLACED (ported render logic removed, now sourced from the clean layer): * `handlers/theory.rs::intdot` — the ported `intdotLayout` `format!` template deleted; now `web_clean::intdot::render_intdot(&html_escape(name), &dotsrc)`. Byte-identical (the `` prefix `Theory: ` has no escapable chars, so escaping `Theory: NAME` vs escaping `NAME` coincide). * `handlers/theory_html.rs::overview_page` — for **local-origin** theories the whole page shell now comes from `web_clean::page::render_page`, with the west (proof-script state) and center (main-view HTML) panes passed as opaque strings (`"{pane} "` — the trailing space the ported template emitted). Byte- identical to the ported local output (verified head/nav/pane/tail against `shell_template`). * `handlers/theory_html.rs` add/delete forms — `add_lemma_html` and `delete_lemma_html` deleted; `path_html` now calls `web_clean::forms::add_form` / `delete_form`. Byte change: the clean forms reproduce HS's stray `</span>` in the `<noscript>` block (byte-exact HS), which the ported code had normalized away. Structural suite unaffected. REMAINS PORTED (kept, headers untouched) — with the precise blocker: * `overview_page` **non-local** branch: `web_clean::page`'s shell bakes in the local-origin header (Reload-file / Append-modified-lemmas actions); a non-local (uploaded) origin gates those off, which the clean shell does not model. Routing it would add the two actions — a behavior change — so the ported template is kept for that branch only. * `edit_lemma_html`: `web_clean::forms::edit_form` hard-codes the textarea `rows="8"`; HS sizes it dynamically (`textHeight = 2 + #newlines` in the lemma plaintext). Routing Edit through the clean form would drop that. Gap in `web_clean::forms` (a fixed `rows` slot); kept ported. * `proof_state` (west pane) and the `path_html` main-view bodies (help / message / rules / sources / proof / tactic) stay in the workspace: these ARE the opaque prover content the clean shell consumes — the prover pretty-prints (via `tamarin_theory`) are data the adapter supplies, not scaffolding. `theory_html.rs` is therefore NOT deleted; it is now the pane/body supplier + the two kept branches above. * JSON envelopes (`handlers/json_resp.rs`) and the 404 page: `web_clean:: envelope` (`{html,title}` / `{redirect}`) and `web_clean::errors:: render_not_found` are validated and byte-compatible on keys, BUT the ported `json_resp` returns `axum::Json<Value>` across ~40 call sites (and carries a third `{alert}` variant `web_clean` does not model), and `not_found_response` is a pervasive minimal-body stub with no request-path in scope. Rewiring either is pure return-type/plumbing churn with no behavior win under the structural gate; left for a follow-up (noted, not forced). -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ## 2. GRAPH/DOT cluster — VENDORED; serializer rewire BLOCKED, reported `graph_clean` is vendored, builds, and passes its round-trip corpus. The DOT **pipeline rewire** (`handlers/dot.rs` `system_to_dot` / `system_to_dot_with` / `render_svg_or_dot_with` -> repr/simplify -> adapter -> graph_clean model -> graph_clean abbrev -> graph_clean serialization) is **not** performed: it cannot be a thin adapter without a behavior change, blocked by three concrete gaps between `graph_clean`'s model and the payload the ported serializer produces. Per protocol ("where the clean crate lacks a behavior, leave the old path in place and report the gap; never patch clean code with old-code logic"), the ported `handlers/dot.rs` and `graph/abbreviation.rs` are kept intact (headers untouched). `graph/abbreviation.rs` is therefore NOT deleted. Blockers (each independently fatal to a byte-faithful, thin-adapter rewire): 1. **Term rendering / abbreviation are one coupled behavior the clean crate deliberately omits.** `graph_clean` treats record-cell text as pre-rendered (BEHAVIOR.md §3a `[GAP]`: full solver term pretty-printer, incl. the `renderRow`/`renderBalanced` width-proportional record-row wrapping, is out of scope), and its `abbrev` names/substitutes over its OWN independent `term::Term` model. The ported cell text is rendered from `LNTerm` with the HS-faithful balanced wrapping AND abbreviations substituted in-place (`apply_abbreviations_fact`, `LNTerm`-keyed). `graph_clean::abbrev` cannot substitute a chosen name into `LNTerm`-rendered text; using it for selection would force routing all term rendering through `graph_clean::Term`, losing the balanced wrapping — exactly the behavior the protocol says to keep. So both the cell renderer and `graph/abbreviation.rs` must stay ported. 2. **No representation for missing-node shapes.** `graph_clean::model::NodeKind` is `Record | Ellipse | Plain`, and `Ellipse` emits `shape="ellipse"` only. HS "missing" nodes are `trapezium` / `invtrapezium` (`dotConcC`/`dotPremC`). A constraint system whose edges reference an absent node cannot be modeled; extending the model would be patching clean code (forbidden). 3. **HS node/port-id allocation is not modeled.** `graph_clean` takes node ids and record ports verbatim (its round-trip builds them from captured DOT); it does not generate HS's graph-global `<n0>,<n1>,…` port scheme (`Text.Dot` `cacheState`/`dsPrems`/`dsConcs`). Reproducing byte-exact HS ids requires that allocation logic, which lives in the ported serializer being "deleted" — writing it into the adapter would transplant replaced logic. Consequence: a rewire that kept the ported cell renderer + abbreviation + missing-node fallback + id scheme would GROW `handlers/dot.rs` (adapter on top of a still-live serializer) rather than thin it, and would not be byte-faithful — the opposite of the task's goal. Recommended: a `graph_clean` round-2 that (a) adds arbitrary `shape=` support (trapezium), (b) exposes an HS port/node-id allocator, and (c) either a `LNTerm`->`Term` bridge or an accepted term-render GAP; then the thin-adapter rewire becomes possible. One isolated sub-component IS cleanly routable today — the legend table via `graph_clean::abbrev:: legend_html` (byte-exact, incl. the 65-space hang indent the ported legend omits) — but wiring only it into the otherwise-kept ported serializer mixes two escaping/whitespace regimes for no net structural gain, so it is left for the round-2 rewire. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ## 3. LOC delta * Added (vendored clean sources, headerless): `graph_clean` 837 + `web_clean` 1005 = **1842 LOC**. * Adapter glue authored in kept files (headerless additions within headered files): ~35 LOC (theory.rs intdot call, overview_page local branch, path_html arms, lib.rs module decls). * Deleted (GPL-headered ported render logic): `add_lemma_html` (~22) + `delete_lemma_html` (~21) + ported `intdot` `format!` template (~15) ≈ **58 LOC**. Tracked-file diff: +82 / -70 across `theory.rs`, `theory_html.rs`, `lib.rs`. * Not deleted (blocked/kept, reported above): `handlers/dot.rs` (2266), `graph/abbreviation.rs` (whole), `theory_html.rs` non-local + edit + pane suppliers. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ## 4. Validation (all green) * `cargo build --workspace` — 0 errors. * `cargo test -p tamarin-server` — lib 80 (incl. 19 vendored `web_clean`), routes_autoprove 6, routes_basic 19, routes_graph 4, routes_proof_step 3, routes_static 3, routes_stubs 15, routes_upload 3; doctest 1 ignored. 0 failures. The captured-HS-response parity fixtures (routes_basic JSON-key + structural, routes_stubs) stay green. * `cargo test -p tamarin-parser` — all green (67 + 2). * Graph fixture tests (`graph-clean` crate): 16 round-trip + 7 abbrev + 1 doctest, 0 failures. Vendored copy verified byte-identical modulo path fixes. ================================================================================ # Dirty-room integration report — units C, D, E, F, G Date: 2026-07-17. Integrator: dirty-room (adapters only; no logic transplanted from replaced files into clean code). Same protocol as above. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ## C. Wellformedness round-3 — RE-SYNCED (part 1) + KEEP-AND-REPORT (part 2) ### C.1 Re-sync of clean sources into `crates/tamarin-parser/src/wf/` — DONE Re-applied the established mechanical path-fix recipe (`crate::{pretty,report, formula,checks}` -> `super::…`; `crate::ast` kept, it resolves to the real tamarin-parser AST) to the round-3 clean sources: * `wf/checks.rs` <- wf-clean/src/checks.rs (grew 46.7K->61.2K: adds `fact_capitalization`, `formula_terms_reducible`/`formula_terms`, the reducible de-Bruijn term renderer, the round-3 guardedness two modes + wrapped printer). * `wf/formula.rs` <- wf-clean/src/formula.rs (adds `pp_formula_wrapped`, the HughesPJ-style multi-line layout engine). * `wf/pretty.rs`, `wf/report.rs` <- byte-identical clean sources (only `crate::ast`, kept). * `wf/mod.rs` rebuilt from wf-clean/src/lib.rs with the two transforms (`pub mod ast;` dropped; `pub use ast::*;` -> `pub use crate::ast::*;`) and the PRESERVED workspace lines `pub mod order; pub use order::*;` re-appended. `wf/order.rs` untouched (workspace-authored). Validation: `cargo test -p tamarin-parser` green (67 + 2 wellformedness); oracle =====END REPORT=====

1 # BEHAVIOR.md — observed spec of the tamarin web-UI constraint-system graph payload 2 3 All statements below are derived from OBSERVED oracle output: the 81 captured 4 crawl manifests under `oracle/captured_responses/` (14705 graph payloads) and 5 targeted live probing of the black-box HS binary (see QUERIES.log). No 6 tamarin-prover source was read. Where a behavior could not be pinned down 7 black-box it is marked **[GAP]**. 8 9 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 10 ## 1. Transport / payload family 11 12 A crawl manifest is JSON: `{base, lemmas, log, capped, manifest}` where 13 `manifest` maps a URL to `{kind, status, body}` (kind ∈ html|text|json|dot). 14 15 Graph-bearing URL families (theory index `N` replaces `#` in captures): 16 17 | URL family | kind | payload | 18 |---|---|---| 19 | `/thy/trace/N/interactive-graph-def/proof/[/...]` | `dot` | **the graph** — graphviz DOT text (`text/plain`) | 20 | `/thy/trace/N/intdot/proof/[/...]` | `html` | wrapper page embedding `` | 21 | `/thy/trace/N/main/cases/{raw,refined}/i/j` | `json` | `{html,title}`; html contains `` | 22 23 The DOT text at `interactive-graph-def` is the whole deliverable. `intdot` is a 24 fixed HTML shell (only the `` and the `dotsrc` path vary). The graph is 25 rendered client-side from the DOT by `/static/js/intdot-graph.es.js`. 26 27 **JSON graph payload** [GAP]: the binary exposes a *separate* `--with-json` 28 rendering backend (`tamarin-prover interactive --help`). No JSON rendering tool 29 is installed here and no `interactive-graph-def` body in the corpus is JSON — 30 the entire corpus is the DOT backend. JSON graph format is therefore unobserved. 31 32 **Graph options** [RESOLVED §8]: the graph URL *does* take query parameters, but 33 they are cookie-driven and added by the UI JS — `simplification=N` (level, default 34 2, always sent), plus the flags `uncompact=`/`uncompress=` (sent at level 0), 35 `unabbreviate=`, `no-auto-sources=`, `clustering=true`. The earlier 36 "byte-identical" observation was because varying only `simplification=1/2/3` does 37 not change output on any probed graph (see §8). The real transforms are triggered 38 by the boolean flags. See §8. 39 40 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 41 ## 2. DOT document structure 42 43 Two headers occur, selected by a single rule (§4): 44 45 ### 2a. Simple header (non-clustered) 46 ``` 47 digraph "G" { 48 nodesep="0.3"; 49 ranksep="0.3"; 50 node[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica",width="0.3",height="0.2"]; 51 edge[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica"]; 52 <statements…> 53 54 } 55 ``` 56 57 ### 2b. Compact header (clustered) 58 ``` 59 digraph "G" { 60 nodesep="0.8"; 61 ranksep="0.8"; 62 sep="4"; 63 splines="true"; 64 overlap="false"; 65 pack="true"; 66 packmode="cluster"; 67 concentrate="true"; 68 compound="true"; 69 remincross="true"; 70 mclimit="10"; 71 nslimit="20"; 72 nslimit1="20"; 73 ordering="out"; 74 rankdir="TB"; 75 showboxes="false"; 76 clusterrank="local"; 77 node[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica",width="0.3",height="0.2",margin="0.05,0.05",shape="ellipse"]; 78 edge[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica",penwidth="1.5",arrowsize="0.5",color="black",style="solid",weight="8"]; 79 <statements…> 80 81 } 82 ``` 83 84 ### Block / whitespace rule (byte-exact) 85 Every block (`digraph … {`, `subgraph … {`, anonymous `{`) is emitted as: 86 `OPEN "\n"` then each inner statement as `STMT "\n"`, then one blank line, then 87 `}`. i.e. the body always ends `…\n\n}`. The top-level digraph is followed by a 88 final `\n`. Subgraph/anonymous blocks are themselves statements of their parent 89 (so the parent supplies the `\n` after their closing `}`). There are **no** blank 90 lines between sections (header attrs / nodes / edges / subgraphs run together); 91 the only blank line in each block is the one before its own `}`. 92 An empty graph is header attrs + blank + `}` (154 bytes). 93 94 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 95 ## 3. Node & edge syntax 96 97 Only three node shapes appear in proof graphs: `record`, `ellipse`, `plain`. 98 99 ### 3a. Record node (a rule / graph-node instance) 100 ``` 101 n<ID>[shape="record",label="{{<p> cell|<p> cell}|{<p> cell}|{<p> cell|<p> cell}}",fillcolor="#RRGGBB",style="filled",fontcolor="black|white",role="<Role>"]; 102 ``` 103 - Attribute order is fixed: `shape,label,fillcolor,style,fontcolor,role`. 104 - Label is a graphviz record: three top groups `{prem}|{info}|{concl}` (a group 105 may be absent/empty). Each group = cells joined by `|`. Each cell = 106 `<portid> text` (port id like `n0`, a space, then the fact text). Ports are 107 numbered per-graph; a record's own node id is allocated AFTER its port ids. 108 - Record-label text escaping: `<`→`\<`, `>`→`\>`, literal `{ } |`→`\{ \} \|`; 109 long facts are wrapped with `\l` (left-justified break) and indented with 110 ` ` runs. (The exact wrap width / term pretty-printing is **[GAP]** — it 111 is graphviz-layout / solver-term-renderer dependent; the model treats cell 112 text as pre-rendered.) 113 - `fillcolor` is a deterministic per-rule color (same rule name ⇒ same color 114 within a graph). `role="Undefined"` for plain multiset-rewriting rules; 115 otherwise the process/agent role. `fontcolor` is `black` on the light MSR 116 palette, `white` on the saturated per-role cluster palette. Exact color hash 117 is **[GAP]**; the model carries colors explicitly. 118 119 ### 3b. Ellipse node (atomic / knowledge / action / temporal node) 120 ``` 121 n<ID>[label="<text>",shape="ellipse"]; 122 n<ID>[label="<text>",shape="ellipse",color="<c>"]; 123 ``` 124 - Attribute order: `label,shape[,color]`. Optional `color` (`gray` for `!KU` 125 intruder-knowledge nodes, `darkblue` for action/event nodes, `gray30`, …). 126 - Ellipse text is NOT record-escaped (`<`,`>` appear literally, e.g. 127 `Session( 'I', <pk(~ltkA), pkB>, … )`). 128 129 ### 3c. Edge 130 ``` 131 n<S>[:<sport>] -> n<D>[:<dport>][<attrs>]; 132 ``` 133 Endpoints optionally carry a `:port` (record port). `<attrs>` is a 134 comma-separated attribute list, in observed key order. Frequent styles: 135 136 | attrs | meaning (inferred from context) | 137 |---|---| 138 | `color="red",style="dashed"` | intruder-deduction / `!KU` edge | 139 | `style="bold",weight="10.0",color="gray50"` | structural premise/conclusion edge | 140 | `style="bold",weight="10.0"` | structural edge (uncolored) | 141 | `color="gray30"` | message/std edge | 142 | `color="blue3",style="dashed"` | temporal-order edge | 143 | `color="orangered2"` | (deduction variant) | 144 | `color="black",style="dashed"` | before/less-than temporal edge | 145 | `style="invis"` | ranking edge to the legend node | 146 | `color="green",style="dotted"` / `purple`/`darkorange3` dashed | other goal edges | 147 148 ### 3d. Node shapes (§ Round-3 item 2) 149 Corpus census (all 12 022 dot + 81 manifests): only `record`, `ellipse`, `plain`. 150 **No trapezium appears in the crawl** — because the crawl captured only *proof* 151 graphs (solved states). Live probing (NAXOS_eCK, NSLPK3 case-distinction graphs) 152 adds one more shape: 153 - **`invtrapezium`** — a `(#var, idx)` placeholder for an **unresolved graph node** 154 referenced by a still-open premise, e.g. `n9[label="(#i, 0)",shape="invtrapezium"]`. 155 `#var` is the (temporal) node variable, `idx` the premise index; the node is fed 156 by a structural edge from the conclusion that must satisfy that premise 157 (`n5:n2 -> n9[style="bold",weight="10.0",color="gray50"]`). Appears in both the 158 compressed default and the uncompressed variant, in every source-case graph 159 tested. Attr order = ellipse's (`label,shape`). 160 - **`trapezium`** (spec-named dual) was **NOT observed** in any probe (all case 161 graphs of two theories, compressed + uncompressed) nor the corpus. Recorded as 162 unobserved; the serializer accepts any shape string so it can be emitted if a 163 caller supplies one. 164 165 ### 3e. Node-id / port allocation (§ Round-3 item 3) — byte-verified over 12 022 166 One global monotonic counter, ids handed out in **emission order** so file order == 167 id order. For each node in order: 168 - a **record** takes one id per cell (in cell order: premises, then the info cell, 169 then conclusions) and **then** one id for the node itself — so a `k`-cell record 170 occupies `n<p>…n<p+k-1>` (ports) and `n<p+k>` (node); 171 - every other kind (ellipse / plain / invtrapezium) takes exactly one id. 172 173 Example: a 4-prem/1-info/3-concl record takes ports `n0…n6`, node `n7`; the next 174 ellipse is `n8`. Verified 12022/12022 in `mine_ids.py` and again in Rust 175 (`tests/alloc_corpus.rs`, via `NodeIdAllocator`). 176 177 ### 3f. Record-cell term rendering + line wrapping (§ Round-3 item 1) 178 **Group structure** (byte-verified over 160 409 records): the label is 179 `{grp}|{grp}|{grp}` where the groups are the *non-empty* ones among 180 `[premises, info, conclusions]`, in that order; the **info** group is always 181 present (100 %). An empty premise or conclusion group is dropped (a source rule 182 renders `{info}|{concl}`). 183 - **Info cell**: `#<temporal> : <RuleName>` and, if the rule has action facts, 184 `[<action>, …]` (e.g. `#i : I_Complete[Complete( … ), …]`, or bare `#vf.5 : Fresh`). 185 - **Premise/conclusion cell**: a single fact. 186 - **Fact spacing**: a fact pads — `Name( a, b )`, and `Name( )` with no args; 187 a **function application** does not — `f(a, b)`; a **tuple** is `<a, b>`. Verified 188 against the corpus. 189 - **Escaping**: inside a record label, `< > { } |` each get a leading backslash; 190 everything else (quotes, `~ $ ^ * ⊕`, spaces) is literal. 191 - **Line wrapping FORMAT** (byte-exact). When a group breaks, physical lines are 192 separated by `\l` (with a trailing `\l`), and each continuation line is indented 193 with a ` ` run **equal to the column of the broken group's first element** 194 (just after `( ` for a fact, after `<` for a tuple, after `[` for an action 195 list). Verified across 188 192 wrapped cells. 196 - **Line wrapping DECISION — RESOLVED (Session 4): a fixed-width paragraph fill, 197 width = 87 columns, measured per fact from column 0.** The Round-3 claim ("first 198 line 5..87, not a flat width, not derivable") is **refuted**: it *is* a flat 199 width. Established by driving crafted single-node theories 200 (`Out(<'a01', …, 'aN'>)`, `Out(<'aa…a', 'y'>)`) through the live server and 201 sweeping the term width one column at a time: 202 - **W = 87 absolute, functor-invariant.** A fact stays on one line iff its flat 203 rendering is ≤ 87 columns; at 88 it breaks. Tested with functor names of length 204 2, 3, 6, 10 (`Ba`,`Out`,`Fact12`,`Factabcdef`): the boundary was **always** at 205 flat width 87 fits / 88 breaks, so the budget is counted from the functor's own 206 column 0, *including* the functor name (a longer name simply fits fewer args). 207 Corpus-consistent: 99.2 % of 190 044 physical lines have fact-content ≤ 87; the 208 residual are single unbreakable atoms wider than 87 (which overflow verbatim). 209 - **Greedy paragraph fill.** When a group overflows, elements pack left-to-right; 210 the separator `, ` trails the element it follows and stays on the line, and the 211 next element wraps when it would pass column 87. Continuation lines use the 212 §3f indent. Example `Out(<'a01'…'a12'>)` (flat 91): line 0 = 213 `Out( <'a01', … 'a11', ` (eleven elements + trailing `, `, ending col 83), 214 `'a12'>` on the next line, then the fact's `)` on its own line. 215 - **Delimiter peel.** The fact's closing `)` peels onto its own physical line at 216 the **functor column** (col 0) whenever the fact breaks; a tuple's `>` peels to 217 the **`<` column** when the last element fills the line 218 (`Out( <'aa…(74)', 'y'` │ `>` │ `)`); an unbreakable atom wider than the budget 219 overflows and only the trailing delimiters wrap. 220 - **KNOWN RESIDUAL** (`fsep` lookahead): the underlying combinator's one-element 221 lookahead lets a *continuation* line hold **one more** element than the first 222 line at the same start column (first line 11, continuation 12 for the 5-col 223 `'aNN'` elements). The width (87), the top-level fit, the first-line packing and 224 the peel columns are byte-verified; reproducing the ±1 continuation lookahead 225 and the boundary peel byte-for-byte would require the exact `fsep`/`nest` doc 226 tree. `graph-clean::render` implements `FILL_WIDTH=87`, `fits_one_line`, and 227 `paragraph_fill` (first-line-exact), tested against these captured probes. 228 229 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 230 ## 4. Clustering / simplification (§ priority 3) 231 232 **Trigger rule (byte-verified over all 14705 graphs, 0 violations):** 233 > The COMPACT clustered rendering (§2b, with `subgraph "cluster_…"` blocks) is 234 > used **iff the graph contains at least one node with `role` ≠ `"Undefined"`.** 235 > Otherwise the SIMPLE header (§2a) is used. 236 237 Non-Undefined roles arise from process/SAPIC agents (e.g. `Initiator`, 238 `Responder`, `server`, `Tag`, `v`). Pure multiset-rewriting theories emit the 239 simple header everywhere; role-annotated theories emit the compact header on 240 every non-empty graph (empty graphs have no role nodes ⇒ simple). 241 242 ### Cluster block 243 ``` 244 subgraph "cluster_<Role>_Session_<k>" { 245 nodesep="0.6"; 246 ranksep="0.6"; 247 label="<Role>_Session_<k>"; 248 style="filled"; 249 color="#RRGGBBAA"; 250 penwidth="2"; 251 fillcolor="#RRGGBBAA"; 252 overlap="false"; 253 sep="4"; 254 n<ID>[shape="record",label="…",fillcolor="#RRGGBB",style="filled",fontcolor="white",role="<Role>"]; 255 256 } 257 ``` 258 - Cluster name = `cluster_<clusterlabel>`; `label` = the clusterlabel 259 (`<Role>_Session_<k>`). Nodes grouped by (role, session instance). 260 - `color`/`fillcolor` are an 8-hex ARGB (alpha `4C`, ~30%) hashed from the 261 cluster; node `fillcolor` is the saturated per-role color. Exact hash **[GAP]**. 262 263 ### What "simplification" means here 264 There is no exposed simplification *level*; the corpus reflects one fixed 265 rendering pipeline. The observable "simplification" is the fixed compression the 266 server applies before rendering: intruder-knowledge (`!KU`) nodes are drawn as 267 gray ellipses; single-premise/conclusion structural chains are drawn with bold 268 weight-10 edges; role nodes are packed into per-session clusters (compact mode). 269 Reproducing the *content* of that compression from a raw constraint system 270 requires the solver and is **[GAP]** — out of scope for a rendering crate. 271 272 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 273 ## 5. Node abbreviation (§ priority 2) 274 275 When a graph is drawn, sufficiently complex sub-terms are replaced by short 276 NAMES and listed in a legend node. Observed from 113 924 legend rows. 277 278 ### 5a. Legend node 279 A `plain` node with an HTML-like label, placed in a sink-rank block: 280 ``` 281 { 282 rank="sink"; 283 n<ID>[shape="plain",label=<<TABLE BORDER="1" CELLBORDER="0" CELLSPACING="3" CELLPADDING="1"><TR>…row0…</TR> 284 <65 spaces><TR>…row1…</TR> 285 … 286 <65 spaces><TR>…rowN…</TR></TABLE>>]; 287 288 } 289 ``` 290 - Each row: 291 `<TR><TD ALIGN="LEFT" VALIGN="TOP"><FONT COLOR="#000000">NAME</FONT></TD> <TD ALIGN="LEFT" VALIGN="TOP">=</TD> <TD ALIGN="LEFT" VALIGN="TOP">EXPANSION</TD></TR>` 292 (single spaces between `</TD>` and the next `<TD>`). 293 - The first `<TR>` is inline after the `<TABLE …>` tag; every continuation `<TR>` 294 is on its own line indented by **65 spaces = the byte length of the 295 `<TABLE BORDER="1" CELLBORDER="0" CELLSPACING="3" CELLPADDING="1">` opening 296 tag** (graphviz hang-indent of the table's children; independent of node id). 297 - EXPANSION is HTML-escaped: `<`→`<`, `>`→`>`, `&`→`&`. Single quotes 298 and `* ^ ~ $ ⊕` are literal. 299 - The legend node is wired to the rest with `style="invis"` edges (from a couple 300 of real nodes to the legend), emitted AFTER the sink block. 301 302 ### 5b. Naming scheme (byte-verified rule) 303 `NAME = <PREFIX><n>`, n = 1,2,3,… per prefix. 304 `PREFIX` = the first **two alphabetic characters, uppercased**, of the ROOT 305 symbol's *name* (one letter if the name has only one). The root symbol name is: 306 - function application `f(…)` ⇒ `f` (`sign`→SI, `senc`→SE, `hash`→HA, 307 `h`/`h1`/`h2`→H, `KDF`/`kdf1`→KD, `pk`→PK, `aenc`/`aead`→AE, `pmult`→PM, …) 308 - public constant `'c'` ⇒ `c` (`'uninitialized'`→UN, `'F_status'`→FS, 309 `'ho_req_ack'`→HO, `'no_message_state'`→NO — non-letters skipped: F,s→FS) 310 - variable `~v` / `$v` / `v` ⇒ `v` (`~AMF_UE_NGAP_ID`→AM, `~cid_N26`→CI, 311 `~gNB_UE_ID`→GN, `~mid…`→MI, `commitmsg`→CO, `StateChannel`→ST) 312 - infix operator, mapped to its function name then the letter rule: 313 `^`→exp→**EX**, `*`→mult→**MU**, `++`→union→**UN**, `⊕`→xor→**XO**. 314 315 Abbreviations NEST: an inner abbreviated term is referenced by name inside an 316 outer expansion (`KD1='KDF(EX1)'`, `MA1='mac(EX1, SI1)'`, `EX1="'g'^MU1"`, 317 `H1="h(<…, EX2, EX3, EX1, …>)"`). So naming is applied bottom-up. 318 319 Per-prefix numbering is by tamarin's canonical term order, NOT by first 320 appearance in the drawing (observed: `In(EX2)` is the first occurrence yet gets 321 `EX2`, and larger/deeper terms get the lower numbers within a prefix). The exact 322 tie-break total order is **[GAP]** (needs the solver's term ordering); the crate 323 assigns per-prefix numbers over a defined order and matches whenever that order 324 is unambiguous. 325 326 ### 5c. Selection — WHICH sub-terms get abbreviated **[RESOLVED — see REPORT2.md]** 327 328 **Rule (three necessary gates; each confirmed by a controlled black-box probe):** 329 > A sub-term `t` is abbreviated **iff** 330 > 1. `renderLen(t) ≥ 10` (Unicode-scalar length of the fully-expanded surface 331 > rendering, *including* `'…'` quotes and `~`/`$` sigils), AND 332 > 2. `occ(t) ≥ 2` (occurrences over the whole constraint system — graph node 333 > facts **and** the sequent's goals/formulas — edge-shared messages count 334 > once), AND 335 > 3. `t` is **not** a tuple `<…>`. 336 > Naming is bottom-up: nested eligible sub-terms are named first 337 > (`LO1='longarg123'`, then `H1='h(LO1)'`). 338 339 This is a rendered **length** gate, not the *size* or *occurrence* the earlier 340 note guessed at — which is why it looked context-free-yet-unexplained. It 341 cleanly settles the old counter-observations: `sign(<…,'g'^~ekR>,~ltkI)` is ≥10 342 chars (abbreviated); `KDF(z)` is 6 chars (never abbreviated, whatever its count); 343 `mac(z,SI1)` and `'g'^~lkR` are below the length/occurrence thresholds in their 344 graphs. `'g'^~lkR` vs `'g'^~lkR.1` differ because the `.1` copy reaches occ 2 in 345 the system while the other does not. 346 347 **Corpus-exactness (necessary direction):** across all **97 538** legend 348 expansions in **11 564** graphs, the minimum rendered length is exactly **10** 349 (0 below) and **0** entries are top-level tuples. Among terms drawn ≥2× in a 350 graph, **0** abbreviated terms violate len≥10 ∧ ¬tuple. Abbreviated terms with 351 graph-occurrence 1 (7 800) all reach ≥2 once the sequent is counted. 352 353 **Controlled probes (crafted theories, live server; see QUERIES.log):** 354 - `'12345678'` (10 chars, ×2) abbreviated; `'1234567'` (9, ×2) not ⇒ boundary = 10. 355 - `'ABCDEFGH'`/`'onceonly12'` (×1) not abbreviated; `'twicelong1'` (×2) is; 356 a 42-char constant (×1) not abbreviated ⇒ occ ≥ 2 is a hard gate at any length. 357 - `<'aa','bb','cc'>` (18 chars, ×2) not abbreviated while sibling `h('longarg123')` 358 (×2) is ⇒ tuple exclusion + bottom-up nesting. 359 360 **Residual (sufficiency):** for **AC/DH-operator** sub-terms (`^ * ⊕ ++ inv em 361 hp pmult`) tamarin counts occurrences over the *normalised* (AC-flattened, 362 DH-normal-form) term, not the surface rendering, so the same term can be 363 abbreviated in one proof state and not another (`em(hp($A),hp($B))` inline in 364 state `56eeb42d`, `EM1` in `54eb2e2c`). 93 % of apparent exceptions are such 365 AC/DH cases; the rest are validation artifacts from expanding shared 366 abbreviations before counting. Outside AC/DH terms the rule is exact. 367 368 The crate `abbrev::select` implements this rule (`MIN_ABBREV_LEN=10`, 369 `MIN_ABBREV_OCC=2`, tuple-excluded, bottom-up); tests reproduce every probe. 370 Byte-parity of the selection set is claimed for non-AC/DH terms and gated by the 371 caller supplying the full-system term multiset. 372 373 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 374 ## 6. System → graph mapping (§ Round-3 item 4) 375 376 From paired `main/proof` sequents and `interactive-graph-def` graphs, the 377 structural mapping a proof state yields (each point traces to §3d/§3f and the 378 content census; the *content* — which nodes/edges exist — is a solver GAP): 379 - a **rule instance** `#t : Rule[actions]` → a `record` node with groups 380 `{premises}|{info}|{conclusions}` (empty prem/concl dropped, info always kept); 381 - an **intruder-knowledge fact** → a gray `!KU( m ) @ #t` ellipse; 382 - a **protocol action/event** → a darkblue `Fact @ #t` ellipse; 383 - a **compressed intruder rule** → an uncolored `#t : rule` ellipse (§4 compression); 384 - an **unresolved node** referenced by an open premise → an `invtrapezium` 385 `(#var, idx)` (§3d); 386 - **edges** connect conclusion-port → premise-port (structural bold w10 [gray50]), 387 intruder deduction (red dashed), message (gray30), temporal order 388 (blue3/black dashed), etc. — the finite §3c vocabulary; 389 - ids from §3e; header from §4 (role); legend from §5. 390 391 `graph-clean::generate` implements this over an independent input model 392 (`System`/`GraphNode`/`RuleInstance`/`SysEdge`), taking node/edge lists, colors, 393 and cell text as INPUTS. `tests/generate_tests.rs` reproduces a live source-case 394 graph (`nsl_invtrap.dot`) **byte-exact** end-to-end. 395 396 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 397 ## 7. Simplification / abbreviation options (§ Round-3 item 5) 398 399 The UI's "Graph simplification off/L1/L2/L3" menu and abbreviation toggle are 400 cookie-driven and appended by the JS as graph-URL **query params** (mined from 401 `/static/js/tamarin-prover-ui.js`, confirmed by live diffs — QUERIES.log): 402 403 | param | UI sends when | observed effect | 404 |--------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------| 405 | `simplification=N` | always (N=level, default 2) | **inert** — the *number* changes nothing (see below) | 406 | `uncompact=` | level 0 only | node COMPACTION off (lone intruder rule → full record vs ellipse) | 407 | `uncompress=` | level 0 only | system COMPRESSION off (Fresh sources + intruder rules re-expand to records) | 408 | `unabbreviate=` | abbreviate cookie off | abbreviation off (terms inlined, legend block omitted) | 409 | `no-auto-sources=` | auto-sources cookie off | disables auto-source precomputation | 410 | `clustering=true` | clustering cookie on | forces role clustering | 411 412 ### 7a. The L1/L2/L3 distinction — RESOLVED (channel-verified negative, Session 4) 413 414 The Round-3 GAP ("simplification 1/2/3 produced byte-identical graphs") is **not a 415 probing artifact and not a hidden session channel** — it is the genuine server 416 behavior: **there is no L1/L2/L3 distinction; the `simplification=N` *number* is 417 inert for every N.** Established under the session-state lens the follow-up 418 demanded: 419 420 - **Channel.** The served JS (`tamarin-prover-ui.js`, lines 78–100 / 476–500) 421 reads a *client-side* `simplification` cookie (default 2) and converts it into 422 the graph-URL query param; at level 0 it *additionally* appends `uncompact=` + 423 `uncompress=`. Levels 1/2/3 send **only** `simplification=N`. The server emits 424 **no `Set-Cookie` on any response** (root, case-graph, main/cases json, proof 425 method) — there is *no server-side session state*; the param is the only channel. 426 - **Change verified before diffing.** Driving the *flags* through the URL param 427 demonstrably changes the graph (e.g. rich NSLPK3 case `refined/6/5`: default 428 5 668 B → `uncompact=&uncompress=` 7 792 B), and the result is byte-identical 429 whether or not a matching cookie is also sent. So the URL param **is** honored. 430 - **The number is inert.** On that 17-node case graph and on a **39-node, 27 KB** 431 DH/bilinear proof-state graph (`eurosp19-eccDAA` analysed, 432 `proof/functional_correctness/_`), every probe of `simplification` ∈ 433 {0,1,2,3,4,5,10,99} — via URL param, via cookie, and via both together, and 434 crossed with every flag combination — collapses to a **single** md5. `flags_L0` 435 … `flags_L99` are byte-identical. (Second theory NAXOS_eCK case `refined/4/1` 436 reproduces this.) 437 - **Mechanism.** The UI's four-item menu (`for i<4`: off / L1 / L2 / L3) dresses up 438 a *binary*: "off" (level 0) = the UI adds the two flags; "L1/L2/L3" = no flags, 439 and the server never consumes the level integer. The real transforms are the 440 independent boolean flags, each with a distinct, measured effect on the 39-node 441 graph: `uncompact=` 39 nodes→richer records (27 361→30 880 B); `uncompress=` 442 39→**74** nodes (re-expands compressed chains, 31 407 B); `unabbreviate=` drops 443 the legend node (39→38) and inlines terms (36 690 B, 0 legend rows); 444 level 0 (`uncompact`+`uncompress`) = 74 nodes, byte-identical to the two flags 445 sent explicitly. 446 447 `graph-clean::options::Options` models the knobs and reproduces the exact query 448 string; `simplification=N` is carried but is documented inert. The abbreviation 449 on/off transform is applied directly (drop legend / inline). Remaining **GAP** 450 here: the *content* of compress/compact (a solver transform — the caller supplies 451 the already-(un)compressed node set). 452 453 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 454 ## 8. What the crate reproduces vs. gaps 455 456 Reproduced & byte-tested against captured/live payloads: 457 - DOT document assembly: both headers, block/whitespace rule, node lines 458 (record/ellipse/plain/**invtrapezium**), edge lines with ports & attrs, cluster 459 subgraphs, legend sink-block + invis edges, empty graph. Round-trip reproduces 460 **12 022/12 022** corpus payloads byte-exact (`tests/roundtrip.rs`). 461 - **Node-id/port allocation** (§3e): `NodeIdAllocator`; validated 12 022/12 022 via 462 the crate model (`tests/alloc_corpus.rs`). 463 - **Record-cell rendering** (§3f): fact/function/tuple spacing, info-cell shape, 464 group-drop rule, record escaping, the wrap FORMAT (alignment indent + `\l`, 465 reproducing an observed wrapped cell byte-exact), and the wrap **DECISION** — 466 fixed width `FILL_WIDTH=87` with `fits_one_line` (top-level fit, boundary 467 byte-verified) and `paragraph_fill` (first-line packing) in `src/render.rs`. 468 - **System → graph GENERATION** (§6): `generate` over the independent `System` 469 model; reproduces a live source-case graph byte-exact (`tests/generate_tests.rs`). 470 - **Simplification/abbreviation options** (§7): `Options` query-string model; the 471 **L1/L2/L3 level number is proven inert** (§7a) — channel-verified negative. 472 - Abbreviation naming, numbering, legend HTML (65-space indent), and the SELECTION 473 rule (§5c, REPORT2.md), plus the cluster/compact trigger (§4). 474 475 Documented gaps (need the GPL solver or an unavailable backend): 476 - JSON graph backend format (unavailable / not in corpus). 477 - **Record-cell wrap residual** (§3f): the `fsep` one-element lookahead (±1 element 478 on continuation lines) and the exact boundary delimiter-peel; the width (87), 479 top-level fit, first-line packing and peel columns are pinned. 480 - **compress/compact content** (§4, §6): which nodes/edges a raw constraint system 481 yields — a solver transform. (The L1/L2/L3 level distinction is no longer a gap: 482 it is proven non-existent, §7a.) 483 - Per-rule/per-cluster **color hashes** (§3a, §4); the `trapezium` dual (§3d, unobserved). 484 - Abbreviation of AC/DH sub-terms (§5c residual, normalised-form occurrence). 485 - Canonical per-prefix numbering tie-break (§5b); empty-prefix start index. 486

1	//! [`RenderSystem`] — a write-sealed, display-only wrapper around a prover
2	//! [`System`].
3	//!
4	//! The interactive graph-render pipeline (`graph::simplify`,
5	//! `handlers::dot`) works on a **clone** of a prover `System` that it mutates
6	//! for display: it drops entailed order constraints, hides intruder/coerce
7	//! nodes, and transitively reduces the `less` relation.  Those mutations go
8	//! through `content_mut()`, which leaves the system's verified-identity
9	//! `subst_system` stamps meaningless — so a mutated display copy must NEVER be
10	//! fed back into the prover.
11	//!
12	//! `RenderSystem` makes that unrepresentable at the type level: it is
13	//! constructed **only** by [`RenderSystem::from_prover`] (a one-way door from a
14	//! prover `System`), and it exposes the inner `System` **only** by shared/unique
15	//! reference through `Deref`/`DerefMut` — there is deliberately **no**
16	//! `into_inner`, and no accessor hands the inner `System` back by value.  So a
17	//! `RenderSystem` cannot be passed to any prover entry point that consumes a
18	//! `System` by value; the render pipeline is typed with `RenderSystem` from the
19	//! clone-for-render boundary onwards.
20	//!
21	//! Reads (`rs.nodes`, `rs.less_atoms`, …) and the display-only mutators
22	//! (`rs.content_mut()`, `rs.goals_mut()`, `rs.nodes_mut()`, …) keep working
23	//! unchanged via the deref coercions, and any function taking `&System`
24	//! (e.g. `compute_basic_graph_repr`) accepts `&RenderSystem` directly.
25	
26	use tamarin_theory::constraint::system::System;
27	
28	/// A prover [`System`] clone dedicated to graph rendering.  See the module
29	/// docs: constructed one-way via [`RenderSystem::from_prover`], never yields
30	/// its inner `System` by value, so it cannot re-enter the prover.
31	pub struct RenderSystem(System);
32	
33	impl RenderSystem {
34	    /// The ONLY constructor: wrap a (cloned) prover `System` for display
35	    /// mutation.  One-way — there is no inverse that returns the inner
36	    /// `System` by value.
37	    #[inline]
38	    pub fn from_prover(sys: System) -> Self {
39	        RenderSystem(sys)
40	    }
41	}
42	
43	impl std::ops::Deref for RenderSystem {
44	    type Target = System;
45	    #[inline]
46	    fn deref(&self) -> &System {
47	        &self.0
48	    }
49	}
50	
51	impl std::ops::DerefMut for RenderSystem {
52	    #[inline]
53	    fn deref_mut(&mut self) -> &mut System {
54	        &mut self.0
55	    }
56	}
57

content.content:
1	//! The full-page theory-view HTML shell returned by the `overview/*` routes.
2	//!
3	//! Observed structure (constant across theories after substituting the four
4	//! parameters below): a fixed `<head>` of stylesheet/script links, a north
5	//! header bar, then four layout panes — west "Proof scripts", east "Debug
6	//! information" (always empty), and center "Visualization display". The west
7	//! pane embeds the proof-script markup (see [`crate::proofscript`]); the center
8	//! pane embeds the currently-selected main content HTML (the same HTML the
9	//! corresponding `main/*` route returns in its JSON `html` field).
10	//!
11	//! Scaffolding constants in `shell_template` are byte-exact copies of oracle
12	//! output with four `§`-delimited slots: NAME, IDX, VERSION, FILENAME. The
13	//! body has no trailing newline (ends `</html>`). All internal links use the
14	//! resolved numeric theory index, never `#`.
15	
16	use crate::escape::html_escape;
17	use crate::shell_template::{PAGE_MID, PAGE_PREFIX, PAGE_TAIL};
18	
19	/// Parameters that vary between rendered pages.
20	pub struct PageParams<'a> {
21	    /// Theory name, shown in `<title>Theory: NAME</title>`.
22	    pub theory_name: &'a str,
23	    /// Resolved numeric theory index used in every internal URL.
24	    pub index: u64,
25	    /// Tamarin version string shown in the header (e.g. `"1.13.0"`).
26	    pub version: &'a str,
27	    /// Source filename used in the download / append links (e.g. `"foo.spthy"`).
28	    pub filename: &'a str,
29	}
30	
31	/// Render the full theory-view page from the shell parameters plus the already
32	/// rendered west (proof-script) and center (main content) pane inner HTML.
33	pub fn render_page(p: &PageParams, west_inner: &str, center_inner: &str) -> String {
34	    let idx = p.index.to_string();
35	    let prefix = PAGE_PREFIX
36	        .replace("§NAME§", &html_escape(p.theory_name))
37	        .replace("§IDX§", &idx)
38	        .replace("§VERSION§", p.version)
39	        .replace("§FILENAME§", p.filename);
40	    let mut out = String::with_capacity(
41	        prefix.len() + west_inner.len() + PAGE_MID.len() + center_inner.len() + PAGE_TAIL.len(),
42	    );
43	    out.push_str(&prefix);
44	    out.push_str(west_inner);
45	    out.push_str(PAGE_MID);
46	    out.push_str(center_inner);
47	    out.push_str(PAGE_TAIL);
48	    out
49	}

# Workspace lint conventions: two invariants of this port are
# compiler-checked rather than convention-checked.  Every intentional
# exception carries a site-local `#[allow]` with a one-line justification
# stating why that site is safe.

# stdout is the byte-parity surface with the Haskell oracle: any stray
# `println!`/`print!` can perturb `tamarin-prover ... --prove` stdout and break
# byte-identical parity.  Diagnostics must go to stderr (`eprintln!`); real CLI
# output must go through the sanctioned output path in the `tamarin-prover`
# binary (annotated with `#[allow(clippy::disallowed_macros)]`).
disallowed-macros = [
    { path = "std::println", reason = "stdout is the byte-parity surface; use eprintln! or the sanctioned CLI output path" },
    { path = "std::print", reason = "stdout is the byte-parity surface; use eprintln! or the sanctioned CLI output path" },
]

# std `HashMap`/`HashSet` have nondeterministic iteration order, which can reach
# observable output or an `Ord`-sensitive structure and diverge from the
# oracle.  Use `tamarin_utils::FastMap`/`FastSet` (deterministic `FxBuildHasher`
# — note the aliases have their own def-ids, so USES of `FastMap`/`FastSet` are
# NOT flagged, only literal `std::collections::HashMap`/`HashSet` paths), or
# keep std with an `#[allow(clippy::disallowed_types)]` and a justification
# comment stating why iteration order never escapes.
disallowed-types = [
    { path = "std::collections::HashMap", reason = "iteration order can reach output; use tamarin_utils::FastMap/FastSet, or allow with a justification comment" },
    { path = "std::collections::HashSet", reason = "iteration order can reach output; use tamarin_utils::FastMap/FastSet, or allow with a justification comment" },
]

# Workspace-wide lint policy.  These clippy lints are `allow`ed
# deliberately: they flag structural choices inherent to a
# function-for-function port of the Haskell prover, where "fixing" them
# would mean diverging from the Haskell shape or churning signatures /
# memory layout for no behavioural gain:
#   - type_complexity / too_many_arguments: solver entry points thread a
#     ProofContext and rich constraint types that mirror the Haskell ones.
#   - result_large_err / large_enum_variant: the error/goal enums carry
#     the full constraint data; boxing them would ripple through every
#     `?` and constructor without changing behaviour.
#   - ptr_arg: several helpers take `&Vec`/`&mut Vec` intentionally.
#   - should_implement_trait / only_used_in_recursion: a few inherent
#     methods and recursion-threaded params are intentional.
#   - doc_overindented_list_items: many module docs lay out Haskell rule /
#     formula syntax as indented ASCII art under a numbered step; the
#     "overindentation" is deliberate alignment, not a list typo.
[workspace.lints.clippy]
type_complexity = "allow"
too_many_arguments = "allow"
result_large_err = "allow"
large_enum_variant = "allow"
ptr_arg = "allow"
should_implement_trait = "allow"
only_used_in_recursion = "allow"
doc_overindented_list_items = "allow"

# Optimized release profile.  `lto = "fat"` enables cross-crate
# inlining (FunSym match arms in the term crate inlined into hot
# solver paths in tamarin-theory).  `codegen-units = 1` improves LLVM
# optimisation at the cost of compile time.  Measured 10-20 % CPU
# speedup on solver-heavy lemmas (csf17::injectivity, jcs18 dnp3).
[profile.release]
lto = "fat"
codegen-units = 1

# Dirty-room integration report — units A (web) + B (graph), re-probe pass

## A/B.1 NAMING (graph_clean->graph, web_clean->web) — DEFERRED (tied to deletion)

The naming policy renames a clean module onto a ported name *when the ported
module it replaces is deleted*. This pass deletes no ported module (the rewires
below are blocked), so the `_clean` suffixes are kept. Renaming without the
corresponding deletion would leave two modules competing for one name.

## B. GRAPH rewire (system_to_dot -> clean generate) — NOT PERFORMED; blockers

The re-probe DID close three of the previous section-2 blockers: `graph_clean`
now models `invtrapezium` (open-premise targets), reproduces HS's global
`n<K>` node/port allocation (`alloc::NodeIdAllocator`, 12 022/12 022), and pins
the record-cell wrap DECISION (`render::FILL_WIDTH=87`). But routing
`system_to_dot`/`_with`/`render_svg_or_dot_with` through `generate` is still not
a byte-faithful thin adapter, blocked by NEW gaps found this pass in the
`generate` assembly + the two independent term models:

1. **`generate` omits role CLUSTERING.** `generate::System` has no cluster
   concept; `generate()` emits a flat node list and only *infers the compact
   header* from roles — it never emits the `subgraph "cluster_<Role>_Session_k"`
   blocks HS/`dotCluster` produce for every role-annotated (non-`Undefined`)
   theory (BEHAVIOR.md §4). Routing clustered graphs through it would drop the
   cluster subgraphs entirely.
2. **`generate` renders record cells FLAT.** `build_record` uses
   `escape_record(&Fact::render_flat())` / `render_info(...)` — no wrapping —
   even though `render::{paragraph_fill,join_wrapped,fits_one_line}` exist. The
   ported serializer wraps at the observed 87-col fill (`render_balanced`,
   `fix_multi_line_label`). Routing would drop cell wrapping on every wide fact.
3. **No `LastAction`, and only invtrapezium of the missing-node dual.**
   `GraphNode` is `Rule|Knowledge|Action|Compressed|OpenTarget`; the ported
   `LastAtom` node and the conclusion-side `trapezium` missing node (`dotConcC`)
   have no representation. Adding them would be patching clean code (forbidden).
4. **Two independent term models; no lossless bridge.** `generate` takes rule
   facts as `Fact{name, args: Vec<graph_clean::Term>}`; the ported cells are
   rendered from `LNTerm` via the HS-faithful `pretty_lnterm`. `graph_clean::
   Term` is explicitly a minimal model (its own docs: full solver pretty-printer
   out of scope), so `LNTerm -> graph_clean::Term` is not lossless, and
   re-deriving cell text through it would (a) transplant term-rendering and (b)
   still hit blockers 1–3.
5. **Serialization DIALECT differs and there is no in-repo byte oracle.**
   `graph_clean` emits HS-exact `digraph "G" {` + global `<n_k>` ports +
   `{{..}|{..}}` bracketing; the ported serializer emits `digraph G {` +
   `<p0>`/`<c0>` + spaced bracketing (validated by its own parse-and-compare
   gate — handlers/dot.rs KNOWN DIVERGENCES). The server's captured HS graph
   fixtures (`interactive_graph_def.html`, `graph.html`) are ISE pages
   (graphviz absent at capture), so there is NO byte oracle in-repo to validate
   a dialect switch against; the only byte-sensitive graph test
   (`routes_graph::dot_output_for_a_simple_system`) hard-codes the ported
   dialect and would break.

Consequence (unchanged conclusion, refined evidence): kept intact, headers
untouched — `handlers/dot.rs`, `graph/abbreviation.rs`, `graph/repr.rs`,
`graph/simplify.rs`, `graph/options.rs`. Recommended `graph_clean` round-4 to

`model`-level pre-rendered-cell entry that bypasses `Term`).

## A. WEB rewire (ProverOps adapter -> web::dispatch) — NOT PERFORMED; blockers

`web_clean::dispatch::Server<ProverOps>` is vendored, builds, and is ready. But
adopting it as the server's request path is not a thin adapter:

1. **`Route::parse` covers a strict SUBSET.** dispatch handles `main/*`,
   `overview/*`, `autoprove`, `next`/`prev`, `source`/`message`, `intdot`,
   `interactive-graph-def`, and the `edit` POST — and 404s everything else. The
   ported server also serves `/` (root + POST upload), `/static/*`, `download`,
   `reload`, `kill`, `equiv` overview, `del/path`, `verify`, `robots.txt`.
   Replacing the router with `Server` alone 404s those and breaks
   `routes_static`/`routes_upload`/`routes_stubs`.
2. **`Server` OWNS version state.** It holds its own `BTreeMap<index,Theory>` +
   monotonic counter. The ported `state.rs` already owns version management for
   the whole (larger) route set. Driving only the dispatch-covered routes
   through `Server` forks the version map from the routes it does not cover —
   an inconsistency, not an adapter.
3. **`ProverOps` needs ~13 pure fragment-producers extracted from ~4 000 LOC.**
   `meta/source_text/west_pane/main_content(per MainReq)/lemma_source/graph_dot/
   nav_target/apply_method/autoprove/edit_lemma/add_lemma/delete_lemma` each

   `handlers/theory.rs`, `theory_html.rs`, `proof_tree.rs`. Extracting them is a
